ANALYSIS
As noted in our prior letter, the Act prohibits a public official from making or
participating in making a govemmental decision in which the official knows or has reason to
know he or she has a financial interest. (Section 87100.) The primary purpose for the conflict-
of-interest provisions of the Act is to ensure that "public officials, wtrltirer etected or appointed,
perform their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financiai interests
or the financial interests of persons who have supported them. (Seciion 81001(b).) The conflict-
of-interest provisions of the Act apply only to "public officials." A "public official,, is defined as
every member, officer, employee, or consultant of a state or local gore*"rt agency. (Section
82048.)
A "local goveflrment agency" is defined in the Act as "a county, city, or district of any
kind including school district, or any other local or regional political rubOiririor, or any
department, division, bureau, office, board, commission or other agency of the foregoing.,'
(Section 82041.) In addition, Section 87300 of the Act states that;Jelvery agency str*t aaopt
and promulgate a Conflict of lnterest Code" applicable to its "designated L*ltoy""r.,, For tire
pulposes of the Act, "agency" is interpreted to mean any state ag"rcy or local government
agency. (Section 82003; Maas Advice Letter, No. 4-9g-261.)
The original qu-estion presented was whether YSEDC board members are public officials
that are subject to the disclosure requirements of the Act which led us to the initiai inquiry of
whether YSEDC is a public agency. "ln analyzing this question we believe that several criteria

PageT
should be considered, and that the true nature of the entity, not merely its stated purpose, should
be analyzed in determining whether the entity is public oi private witirin the meaning of the
Act." ( In re Siegel (1977) 3 FPPC Ops. 62) The answer controls whether YSEDC is considered
a local govemment agency and required to adopt a separate conflict-of-interest code for its
employees and board members under Section 87300.
The Commission-established criteria for determining whether an entity is governmental
in character are found in its opinion In re Siegel, supra. fhi Siegel factors deiermine whether
local entities are public or private in character. The Commission has applied the following four-
part test:-
(1) Whether the impetus for formation of the entity originated with a government agency.
(2) Whether the entity is substantially funded by, or its primary source of funds is, a
govemment agency.
(3) Whether one of the principal purposes for which the entity was formed is to provide
services or undertake obligations which public agencies are legally authorized to perform and
which, in fact, they traditionally have performed.
(4) whether the entity is treated as a public enrity by other laws.
In our original letter, we concluded that three of the four factors were met and concluded
that YSEDC is a public agency. In this letter, based on the additional information we received
from the member cities, we conclude that all the factors are met and reaffirm our conclusion that
YSEDC is a public agency under the purview of the Act.
1. Did the impe:tusforformation of the entity ortginate with a goyerntnent entity?
Generally, the first factor has been met where an entity is created by some official action
of another govemmental agency. For example, in the Siegel6pinion, although the agency was
created as a nonprofit corporation, the city council was intimatily involved ii the creation of the
corporation in question.
According to the documents submitted by the member agencies, the member agencies
created the YSEDC by joint resolution. The member agencies pledged to support VSEOC Uy

period of not less than three years; and that Sutter and Yuba counties Board of Supervisors shall
designate the newly created Yuba-Sutter Economic Development Corporation to serve as the
administering agent for the proposed Yuba-Sutter Economic Development District.
2 The Commission's subsequent advice letters, and an opinion, state that it is not necessary that all four of
the Siegel factors be satisfied for an entity to be considered a locil government agency. (ln re vor*( l9g I ) 6 FppC
Ops. l; O'Shea AdviceLetter,No.A-91-570.) Itisonlyn"..su.ylhuttheentitisatisfy'enoughof

Therefore, the second criterion of the Siegel test is met.,,
Moreover, even the revenue generated by loan administration appears to be public in

i- Is one of the principal purposesfor which the entity isformed to provide services or

According to your facts, of YSEDC's 23 voting members of the Board, 12 areeither
elected officials from the four member cities and two member counties or appointed by the

However, according to the bylaws, they are nominated by the members ,1ne nelected and

and control the remainder of the membership. Because oi this control by a majority of
governmental officials acting in their official capacities over YSEDC's ultimaie operation, it can
be concluded that YSEDC does serve a public function.
B. Service Tradittonalty Performed by Publtc Agenctes; Secondly, we look at factors

business leaders and facilitation of meetings with developers, planners and other key individuals,
as required, is available. According to your letter, the YSEDC also provides other services to the
community including business recruitment, expansion and retentioniervices, economic research,
financial and technical assistance, loan administration, and special projects. None of these

However, the additional information we received in the letters from the member agencies
is enlightening. One of the primary goals for the creation of the EDC was to foster stable and

4. Is the entity treated as a puhlic entity by other statutory provisions?
The final factor that is considered in evaluating an entity's status under the Act is whether
the entity is treated as a public entity by other provisions of law. The corporation at issue in the
Siegel Opinion was recognized as a public body in both tax and securities law.
Your facts state that YSEDC is a nonprofit entity. However, your facts indicate that
YSEDC has determined that the Ralph M. Brown Act (open meetingiaws) applies to its board
meetings. Consistent with prior advice, the requirement io follow tlie open *e"ti.rg laws weighs
in favor of the fourth criterion of the Siegel analysis. (KranitzAdvice i"tt"., No. 4-03-204;
Stark, supra; Alperin Advice Letter, No. A-95-l lg.)

SUMMARY
ln summary, based on the above discussion, YSEDC is a local public agency within the
meaning of the Act, and will be required to adopt a conflict of interest iode under Section g7300,
A conflict of interest code should be formulated at the "most decentralized level possible.,,
(Section 87301.) An agency is required to submit its initial proposed conflict of interest code for
approval to the code reviewing body no later than six monthi after coming into existence.
(Section 87303.) Since YSEDC is multicounty in jurisdiction (in both Suiter and yuba County),
the Commission would be the code reviewing body.
ln your February 20,2009 communication you expressed concem that as a government
agency you would be required to obtain a completed Form 700 from every director, employee,
committee member and consultant/contractor with whom you do business. However, Section
87302 requires that every conflict of interest code only spicifically enumerate the positions
within the agency which involve the making or participation in the making of decisions which
may foreseeably have a material effect on any tinancial interest. Thus, thJse persons who do not
make or participate in making decisions (such as clerical employees or members of advisory
committees) where recommendations are not regularly followed by the board of directors would
not be designated in the code, and those who make and participate in a limited scope of decisions
(such as IT staff) mjght have narrowly tailored disclosuie obligations in the code fit to their
decisionmaking ability.
Finally, please note, Section87304 provides:
"If any agency fails to submit a proposed conflict of interest code or amendments,
or if any stat-e_ag?1cy fails to report amendments pursuant to subdivision (b) of
Section 87306 within the time limits prescribed pursuant to Section g7303 or
87306, the code reviewing body may issue any appropriate order directed to the
agency or take any other appropriate action, including the adoption of a conflict of
interest code for the agency."

you may be aware, the Commission established criteria for determining whether an entity
is governmental in character. (ln re Stegel (1977) 3 FPPC Ops. 62.) The Commission has
applied the following four-part test:
(l) Whether the impetus for formation of the entity originated with a government
agency
(2) Whether the entity is substantially funded by, or its primary source of funds is, a
govemment agency.
(3) Whether one of the principal purposes for which the entity was formed is to
provide services or undertake obligations which public agencies are legally authorized to
perform and which, in fact, they traditionally have performed.
(4) Whether the entity is treated as a public entity by other laws

The YSEDC board of directors is composed of representatives from both the public

As noted in our prior letter, the Act prohibits a public official from making or
participating in making a govemmental decision in which the official knows or has reason to
know he or she has a financial interest. (Section 87100.) The primary purpose for the conflict-
of-interest provisions of the Act is to ensure that "public officials, wtrltirer etected or appointed,
perform their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financiai interests
or the financial interests of persons who have supported them. (Seciion 81001(b).) The conflict-
of-interest provisions of the Act apply only to "public officials." A "public official,, is defined as
every member, officer, employee, or consultant of a state or local gore*"rt agency. (Section
82048.)
A "local goveflrment agency" is defined in the Act as "a county, city, or district of any
kind including school district, or any other local or regional political rubOiririor, or any
department, division, bureau, office, board, commission or other agency of the foregoing.,'
(Section 82041.) In addition, Section 87300 of the Act states that;Jelvery agency str*t aaopt
and promulgate a Conflict of lnterest Code" applicable to its "designated L*ltoy""r.,, For tire
pulposes of the Act, "agency" is interpreted to mean any state ag"rcy or local government
agency. (Section 82003; Maas Advice Letter, No. 4-9g-261.)
The original qu-estion presented was whether YSEDC board members are public officials
that are subject to the disclosure requirements of the Act which led us to the initiai inquiry of
whether YSEDC is a public agency. "ln analyzing this question we believe that several criteria

a local govemment agency and required to adopt a separate conflict-of-interest code for its
employees and board members under Section 87300.
The Commission-established criteria for determining whether an entity is governmental
in character are found in its opinion In re Siegel, supra. fhi Siegel factors deiermine whether
local entities are public or private in character. The Commission has applied the following four-
part test:-
(1) Whether the impetus for formation of the entity originated with a government agency.
(2) Whether the entity is substantially funded by, or its primary source of funds is, a
govemment agency.
(3) Whether one of the principal purposes for which the entity was formed is to provide
services or undertake obligations which public agencies are legally authorized to perform and
which, in fact, they traditionally have performed.
(4) whether the entity is treated as a public enrity by other laws.
In our original letter, we concluded that three of the four factors were met and concluded
that YSEDC is a public agency. In this letter, based on the additional information we received
from the member cities, we conclude that all the factors are met and reaffirm our conclusion that
YSEDC is a public agency under the purview of the Act.

ln summary, based on the above discussion, YSEDC is a local public agency within the

A conflict of interest code should be formulated at the "most decentralized level possible.,,

the Commission would be the code reviewing body.
ln your February 20,2009 communication you expressed concem that as a government
agency you would be required to obtain a completed Form 700 from every director, employee,

committees) where recommendations are not regularly followed by the board of directors would
not be designated in the code, and those who make and participate in a limited scope of decisions
(such as IT staff) mjght have narrowly tailored disclosuie obligations in the code fit to their
decisionmaking ability.

Section 87306 within the time limits prescribed pursuant to Section g7303 or

agency or take any other appropriate action, including the adoption of a conflict of
interest code for the agency."
